coredata.dc:title: Green color luminescent carbon dots for stability flexible composite sheet and mercury ion detection application
coredata.dc:creator: Jung, Jae Yong
Park, Jin Young
Park, Sung Jun
Yang, Hyun Kyoung
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots with a size of approximately 4 nm were synthesized using water-soluble piperazine and phthalic acid. The synthesized carbon dots exhibited characteristic Raman signals of D-band and G-band, along with an XRD pattern signal from the (002) plane. When excited at 365 nm, they emitted a broad spectrum in the range of 450 nm–650 nm, displaying a cyan-colored light. The synthesized carbon dots could be utilized in both powder and solution forms and could be mixed with PDMS to create flexible composites, allowing for various shapes to be produced. Notably, the flexible composite maintained its luminescent properties even after 40 h of exposure to heat, moisture, and UV light, and demonstrated a 95 % blocking effect on blue wavelengths when applied to blue LEDs. Furthermore, it visualized the characteristics of fingerprints on glass substrates and revealed text and labels visible only under UV light when applied to banknotes and plastic cards, showing potential for crime prevention. Finally, the carbon dots exhibited a sensitive response to mercury ions, with a decrease in luminescent properties, enabling the detection of mercury ions at a level of 0.1516 μM, suggesting various practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Anti-counterfeiting
Fingerprint identification
Thermally stable

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesized of green emission carbon dot nanoparticles
Properties of synthesized carbon dot nanoparticles
Cabon dots applied to various applications

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (grant number: RS-2023-00240874, RS-2023-00237313, and RS-2023-00246326)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Experimental process: Synthesis of Carbon dot.
Characterization of carbon dots: (a) XRD pattern, (b) Raman shift, (c) FT-IR spectrum, and (d) UV–vis spectrum (optical band gap; inset).
(a) PLE and PL spectrum, (b) CIE coordination, (c) PL spectra according to various excitation wavelength, (d) changed the PL intensity, and (e) PL decay curved of carbon dots.
(a) TEM image (high magnitude inset), (b) particle sized distribution histogram, and (c) particle surface inter lattice distance of carbon dots.
XPS spectra; (a) Full survey, (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, and (d) O 1s of carbon dots.
Carbon dot-PDMS flexible composite applied various shape.
(a) UV radiation and hot water resistance test and (b) applied to blue LED chip of carbon dot-PDMS flexible composite.
(a) Thermal stable test, (b) checking color change, and (c) PL spectra change according to the temperature of carbon dot-PDMS flexible composite.
Applied to (a) fingerprinting identification, (b) anti-counterfeiting of carbon dot.
(a) Luminescence changed by metal ions and (b) Hg2+ ion concertation sensitivity of carbon dot solution.
Different decay curve of cardon dots: (a) 0 μM, (b) 50 μM, (c) 100 μM, and (d) bar graph lifetime of Hg2+ ion.

head.ce:title: Green color luminescent carbon dots for stability flexible composite sheet and mercury ion detection application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Marine-Bionics Convergence Technology Center, Pukyong National University, Busan, 48513, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Marine-Bionics Convergence Technology Center, Pukyong National University, Busan 48513, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical, Electronics and Software Engineering, Pukyong National University, Busan, 48513, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Artificial Intelligence Convergence, Pukyong National University, Busan, 48513, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Artificial Intelligence Convergence, Pukyong National University, Busan 48513, Republic of Korea

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Marine-Bionics Convergence Technology Center, Pukyong National University, Busan, 48513, Republic of Korea.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots with a size of approximately 4 nm were synthesized using water-soluble piperazine and phthalic acid. The synthesized carbon dots exhibited characteristic Raman signals of D-band and G-band, along with an XRD pattern signal from the (002) plane. When excited at 365 nm, they emitted a broad spectrum in the range of 450 nm–650 nm, displaying a cyan-colored light. The synthesized carbon dots could be utilized in both powder and solution forms and could be mixed with PDMS to create flexible composites, allowing for various shapes to be produced. Notably, the flexible composite maintained its luminescent properties even after 40 h of exposure to heat, moisture, and UV light, and demonstrated a 95 % blocking effect on blue wavelengths when applied to blue LEDs. Furthermore, it visualized the characteristics of fingerprints on glass substrates and revealed text and labels visible only under UV light when applied to banknotes and plastic cards, showing potential for crime prevention. Finally, the carbon dots exhibited a sensitive response to mercury ions, with a decrease in luminescent properties, enabling the detection of mercury ions at a level of 0.1516 μM, suggesting various practical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), also known as carbon quantum dots (CQDs), have garnered significant attention in recent years due to their unique and versatile properties. CDs are zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials, typically less than 10 nm in size, that exhibit intriguing optical, electronic, and chemical characteristics [1–3]. Their exceptional properties, including strong photoluminescence, biocompatibility, chemical stability, and low toxicity, make them promising candidates for a broad range of applications in fields such as bioimaging, drug delivery, sensors, and optoelectronic devices [4–6]. The synthesis of carbon dots can be achieved through various methods, broadly categorized into top-down and bottom-up approaches. Top-down methods include techniques such as laser ablation, arc discharge, and electrochemical oxidation, where larger carbon structures are broken down into nanoscale particles. Conversely, bottom-up methods involve the assembly of carbon atoms from molecular precursors through processes like hydrothermal or solvothermal synthesis, microwave-assisted synthesis, and pyrolysis [7–10]. Each method offers distinct advantages in terms of control over size, shape, and surface functionality of the resultant carbon dots. One of the most compelling features of carbon dots is their tunable photoluminescence. CDs exhibit size-dependent fluorescence, where variations in size and surface states lead to emission of different colors under ultraviolet or visible light excitation. This tunable photoluminescence, combined with their excellent water solubility and low cytotoxicity, has opened numerous possibilities for their use in biological imaging and diagnostics [11,12]. For instance, carbon dots have been employed in live cell imaging to track cellular processes with high spatial and temporal resolution. Beyond biological applications, carbon dots have demonstrated potential in the development of advanced materials and devices. They have been incorporated into light-emitting diodes (LEDs), solar cells, and photocatalysts, showcasing their ability to enhance the performance and efficiency of these technologies. In environmental sensing, CDs have been utilized as sensitive and selective probes for detecting metal ions, organic molecules, and other pollutants, highlighting their versatility and practical utility [13–15]. Song et al. synthesized low-toxicity carbon dots using sequential synthesis. When excited at 360 nm, they emitted a peak wavelength of 443 nm, and a change in emission intensity was observed with varying pH levels. Additionally, by adding Fe3+ ions, they demonstrated the potential use of these carbon dots as a moisture sensor [16]. Yuan et al. synthesized blue, green, and red-emitting carbon dots using a solvothermal method. The colloidal carbon dots exhibited tunable emission wavelengths based on the types of reagents used, enabling the realization of various colors. They also demonstrated the potential application of these carbon dots as thin-film LED devices by fabricating them into LED components [17]. Jiang et al. extracted carbon dots from commercially available instant coffee powder by dissolving it in hot water. The resulting nanoparticles, approximately 4 nm in size, exhibited blue emissions. These carbon dots were introduced into human hepatocellular carcinoma cells to visualize the cell morphology and incorporated into fish feed to observe the color change in fish that consumed the feed. This demonstrated the potential application of carbon dots in the bioimaging field [18]. Zhao et al. synthesized colloidal carbon dots with a high quantum yield and demonstrated their potential application in solar panels by integrating them into solar concentrators. They also observed and reported changes in the luminescent properties of the carbon dots due to various doping agents [19–21]. In this study, we present a simple and easy synthesis method to create green-emitting carbon dots, which can be utilized in two forms: powder and solution. The synthesized carbon dots were incorporated into a flexible composite, allowing us to observe changes due to external environmental factors such as heat, moisture, light, and temperature, while maintaining their luminescent properties. We demonstrated that these carbon dots can be used for LED color filters and as blue light blockers to protect the eyes. Additionally, we proposed their potential application in anti-counterfeiting and fingerprint detection, contributing to crime prevention. Lastly, we expanded their utility by showing that they can detect hazardous mercury ions among metal ions, thus broadening their range of applications. We emphasized the simple synthesis method and the diverse potential applications of carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesized of green emission carbon dot nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: To synthesize green-emitting carbon dot nanoparticles, precisely measure 23.22 mmol of piperazine (C4H10N2, Sigma-Aldrich) and 6.75 mmol of phthalic anhydride (C8H4O3, Sigma-Aldrich) using an analytical balance. Add these to a beaker containing 10 mL of deionized (D.I.) water. Heat the beaker to 110 °C while stirring to completely evaporate the water, forming a solid. Recover the solid from the beaker and grind it using a mortar and pestle to obtain a fine powder (Fig. 1 ).

Mix 0.1 g of the synthesized carbon dot powder with 10 g of polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS, SYLGARD 184) to create a mixture. Pour the mixture into various molds and cure it in an oven at 80 °C for 1 h. This process produces flexible composite sheets and various composite shapes [22].

Potential application in crime prevention, the synthesized carbon dot powder was sprinkled onto a glass substrate bearing a fingerprint. The fingerprint characteristics were visualized by illuminating the substrate with a UV lamp. The fingerprint was obtained by having the volunteer clean their thumb thoroughly and press it onto a soda-lime glass substrate, with informed consent obtained from the volunteer [23].

Applied to the field of anti-counterfeiting for everyday items, 0.1 g of the synthesized carbon dot powder was dissolved in 10 mL of deionized (D.I.) water, and 1 g of polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, Sigma-Aldrich) was added to prepare an ink formulation. The ink was applied to banknotes and plastic cards, and the samples were then examined under normal light and UV lamp illumination for comparison.

The synthesized solution-state carbon dots were reacted with 40 μM solutions of Fe3+, Pb3+, Cd2+, and Hg2+ to test their potential as metal ion detectors. The changes in the UV lamp-induced fluorescence or the PL spectra were observed. Additionally, the concentration of Hg2⁺ ions was varied (0–100 μM), and the changes in the luminescence intensity of the carbon dot solution were monitored.

The synthesized carbon dot nanoparticles of structural characterization by X-ray diffraction (XRD, PANalytical/X'pert 3-powder) was performed with Cu Kα radiation (
λ =
1.5406 Å). The chemical compositions of the samples were studied by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; KRATOS Analytical Ltd./AXIS SUPRA) using Al-Kα lines. The C1s peak (284.6 eV) was used to calibrate the peak positions of the insulating samples. The surface morphology was investigated using field-emission transmission electron microscopy (FE-TEM; JEOL, JEM-2100F). The absorbances were recorded on an ultraviolet–visible (UV/Vis; Shimadzu/UV 18000) spectrometer. The Raman shifts were observed using a Raman spectrometer (LabRam-HR 800, Horiba Jobin-Yvon). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (JASCO, V-670) with a 150 W xenon lamp as the excitation source and a photomultiplier tube operating at 200 V. Origin Pro 2018 software was used for data fitting and visualization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Properties of synthesized carbon dot nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots synthesized by the reaction of piperazine, and phthalic acid were prepared as a yellow powder visible under normal daylight. When irradiated with a 365 nm UV lamp, the powder emitted green light. The synthesized carbon dot powder was easily soluble in water, and the resulting transparent solution emitted the same green light. To investigate the structural characteristics of these carbon dots, XRD was performed in the range of 10°–80°, and the patterns were shown in Fig. 2(a). Peaks were observed in the broad range of 12°–27°, indicating amorphous carbon peaks [24]. These peaks were similar to the characteristic signals of the (002) plane of graphite, suggesting that the synthesized carbon dots consisted of small crystalline structures with a disordered surface [25]. Additionally, the Raman spectrum of the carbon dots (CDs), shown in Fig. 2(b), displays two characteristic vibrations at 1357 cm−1 and 1568 cm−1. These vibrations correspond to the defect band (D band) and the graphite band (G band), respectively. The D band is linked to the vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds, whereas the G band is associated with the vibration of sp2− bonded carbon atoms within a 2D hexagonal lattice [26]. The relative intensity ratio of these two peaks is approximately 0.272 (ID/IG), indicating the degree of graphitization, which is consistent with the XRD findings. The FT-IR spectrum was employed to further determine the surface functional groups present in the CDs. The absorption peaks observed at 3455 and 3000 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H groups [27], respectively. Peaks at 1486, 1100, and 662 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of CC, C–O–C, and –OH groups [28]. The prominent peak at 1623 cm−1 indicates the presence of amide carbonyls, suggesting a successful conjugation between the amines of piperazine and the carboxyl groups of phthalic acid (Fig. 2(c)).

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the CDs is displayed in Fig. 2(d). In the ultraviolet region, two distinct peaks appear at 257 nm and 346 nm. These peaks are attributed to the π-π∗ transitions associated with sp2-hybridized carbon in a honeycomb lattice and the n-π∗ transitions of the C–N bond [29], respectively. The optical bandgap (E bandgap) of CDs was determined using a Tauc plot using the following equation [30]:
(1)
(
α h ν
)
1 / n
= X
(
h ν −
E
b a n d g a p
)

Here, α is the linear absorption coefficient, hν the photon energy, and X is a proportionality constant. The primary electronic transitions in CDs governing the excitation and recombination of electrons within the bands are driven by directly allowed transitions with n = 0.5. The calculated bandgap of the synthesized CDs was calculated to be π-π∗ transitions was 4.28 eV and n-π∗ transitions was 3.27 eV (Fig. 2(d) inset).

Fig. 3 (a) displays the photoluminescence excitation (PLE) and photoluminescence emission (PL) spectra of the CDs. When excited at a wavelength (λex) of 420 nm, the CDs exhibited a maximum emission wavelength (λem) around 500 nm. According to the Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE, Fig. 3(b)), the coordinates derived from the PL spectrum of the CDs were (0.2043, 0.4644), indicating a cyan-green color. When the particle size decreases, even if the composition remains the same as in the bulk state, the electrical and optical properties change due to the quantum confinement effect. In the bulk state, carbon does not emit light after absorbing external light energy. However, when the carbon is reduced to the nanoscale, the quantum confinement effect allows it to absorb external light energy, causing electrons in the valence band to become excited and move to the conduction band. As these electrons return to the ground state, the emitted energy produces visible light. Due to this phenomenon, nanosized carbon dots synthesized in this study absorb light energy in the UV region and emit a green luminescence spectrum. The fluorescent emission of the CDs is influenced by the λex, and the PL spectra were recorded at various excitation wavelengths (Fig. 3(c) and (d)). As the λex increased, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs initially rose and then diminished. Notably, the PL emission peak of the CDs did not shift towards the red region as λex increased. Studies have reported that the emission wavelength of synthesized carbon dots can undergo a red shift with changes in excitation wavelength, which is attributed to numerous surface bindings on the synthesized carbon dots [31]. Conversely, in cases where there is no shift in the emission wavelength, this phenomenon is due to chemically stable bonds and fewer surface defects, as observed in carbon dots functionalized with the 2,3-diaminophenazine group [32]. In this study, the synthesized carbon dots exhibited relatively fewer surface bindings, leading to the characteristic of unchanged emission wavelength despite variations in the excitation wavelength. The quantum yield of the synthesized CDs were about 17 % (Solution type) and 31 % (powder type) (Fig. S2).

The mono exponential fitting function (Eq. (2)) was utilized to fit the PL decay (excitation and emission wavelengths are 375 nm and 500 nm, respectively) curves of the carbon dot nanoparticles [33]:
(2)
I
( t )
= I
( o )
exp
( ‐ t / τ )
+ A
where τ indicates lifetime, I(o) denotes constant value and I(t) the luminescence strength at time t correspondingly. The measured decay lifetime was 6.34 ns (Fig. 3(e)), indicating that the luminescence mechanism of our green-emitting CDs is characterized by radiative recombination due to the short duration [34].

FE-TEM analysis was conducted to assess the particle size and shape of the synthesized CDs, as illustrated in Fig. 4 . According to Fig. 4(a), the synthesized CDs exhibit a round honeycomb structure. The average particle size is approximately 4.2 nm, as depicted in the histogram in Fig. 4(b). The nanoparticle size distribution is described by the formula below [35]:
(3)
f
( D )
=
(
1
2 σ
D
)
exp
[
‐
In 2
(
D /
D 0
)
/
2 σ
2
]

Here, D represents the average particle size, and σD denotes the standard deviation. A typical fitting of the log-normal distribution function to the particle size distribution histogram was performed using Origin software [36]. High-magnification images confirmed the lattice pattern of the particles, and the inter-lattice distance was measured to be 0.315 nm (Fig. 4(c)). This distance is consistent with the lattice constant calculated from the position signal of the (002) plane for a graphitic framework [37].

XPS analysis was conducted to determine the chemical bond energy states of the synthesized CDs, and the results are presented in Fig. 5 . As shown in Fig. 5(a), the full-scan survey spectrum identified peak signals at the 1s positions of O, N, and C. High-resolution spectra for each element were also obtained for a more detailed analysis. The Gaussian-fitted C 1s spectra indicated the presence of sp2 carbon (C–C and CC; 282.13 eV), carbon-nitrogen (C–N and CN; 283.23 eV), carbonyl (CO; 284.86 eV), and carboxylate (O–CO; 286.30 eV) groups on the surface of the CDs (Fig. 3(b)). In Fig. 3(c), nitrogen species such as pyridinic N (396.96 eV), pyrrolic N (398.51 eV), and graphitic N (400.08 eV) were detected, indicating the incorporation of nitrogen into the CDs. The O1s spectra, shown in Fig. 3(d), were deconvoluted into three Gaussian peaks corresponding to the CO groups of functionalized species (528.19 eV) and C–O bonds of aromatic carbon (529.12 eV) [38,39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cabon dots applied to various applications
ce:section.ce:para: To explore various applications, the synthesized carbon dot powder, which exhibits green fluorescence under UV light, was first mixed with PDMS to prepare a slurry. The slurry was then poured into molds of diverse and intricate shapes such as circles, squares, hearts, stars, letters, models, and QR codes, and cured. As shown in Fig. 6 , the cured CDs-PDMS composites replicated the mold shapes accurately, appearing yellow under normal light but emitting a distinct green fluorescence when exposed to 365 nm UV light. Additionally, the fabricated composites could be easily bent, folded, and rolled by hand, stretched when pulled, and exhibited transparency when made thin. The potential applications of flexible composites with various complex shapes were demonstrated. A video of the flexible composite that can be easily bent and folded by hand is shown in Fig. S3.

Evaluation of the stability fabricated composites against light and moisture, they were continuously exposed to a 365 nm UV lamp and placed in a beaker of water in an 80 °C oven. The changes in luminescence intensity over time were measured and are shown in Fig. 7 (a). The composites exposed to the UV lamp showed a 50 % decrease in luminescence intensity after 12 h (inset image), with no significant changes observed from 30 to 40 h (Fig. 7(a) left). Although a color change from yellow to ivory was observed under normal light after 40 h, the composites retained their characteristic green fluorescence when exposed to UV light, despite a reduction in intensity detectable by spectrum analysis. Composites exposed to water and 80 °C for prolonged periods showed a 50 % decrease in luminescence intensity at 15 h (inset image), maintaining this level with a little further decrease up to 40 h (Fig. 7(a) right). These composites exhibited less color change compared to those exposed to the UV lamp but turned slightly paler than the original samples. However, they maintained their characteristic green fluorescence under UV light, demonstrating resistance to both UV light and moisture. When applied to a 450 nm LED chip, these composites reduced blue light and emitted a green luminescence spectrum, as shown in Fig. 7(b), suggesting their potential use as green light-emitting devices or as filters to protect eyes by blocking blue light.

The fabricated green luminescent composites in devices such as LED chips, it is essential that they exhibit minimal color and luminescence changes due to heat generated by the device. The composites were subjected to direct heat, and the luminescence changes with temperature were observed and presented in Fig. 8 (a). When the composites were heated using a hot plate, thermal imaging revealed a color change from red to white as the temperature increased, indicating the temperature distribution. From 25 °C to 200 °C, the composites showed no significant visual color change or decrease in luminescence intensity under UV light. Furthermore, color extraction from the photographs, shown in Fig. 8(b), indicated that there were no significant color changes with increasing temperature. The fabricated composite did not show a noticeable decrease in its characteristic green emission under UV lamp irradiation with increasing temperature. However, the PL spectrum revealed a decrease in emission intensity with rising temperature, as shown in Fig. 8(c). Specifically, at 100 °C, the emission intensity decreased by 16 %, but this was not a continuous decrease; the emission intensity remained stable at high temperatures. This is attributed to the stable structure of the carbon dots synthesized and their composite formation with the polymer PDMS, which reduces the effects of thermal degradation. Such flexible composites can be directly applied to LED chips and used as low-temperature thermal sensing optical sensors in the range from room temperature to 100 °C.

The synthesized carbon dot powder applied to crime prevention, it was scattered on a glass substrate with a fingerprint and then exposed to a UV lamp, as shown in Fig. 9 (a). The thumbprint of the subject was clearly visualized, and upon magnification, features such as cores, bifurcations, terminations, islands, sweat pores, hooks, and creases were observable. The unique green fluorescence of the synthesized carbon dots made it easy to visualize and identify the fingerprint characteristics. The mechanism for imaging fingerprints could be similar to the conventional powder technique, relying on electrostatic interactions between the charged CDs surface functional groups (amine, amide, and carboxylic) and the moisture, basic proteins, or oily components of the fingerprint residue. Wang et al. synthesized solution-state blue luminescent carbon dots using a microwave-assisted method and then mixed them with methanol to produce a carbon dot powder through freeze-drying. They applied this carbon dot powder to visualize fingerprints on glass substrates, plastic bottle caps, and mobile phone screens by sprinkling it on the prints and irradiating them with a UV lamp [40]. The fingerprints became visible with blue luminescence, and the characteristics of these luminescent fingerprints were observed, demonstrating the potential application of this method in fingerprint detection. As another method for crime prevention, anti-counterfeiting ink was applied to banknotes and cards, as shown in Fig. 9(b). Under normal light, the applied ink was not visible to the naked eye, but under a UV lamp, the letters ‘PKNU’ became clearly visible. The unique fluorescence of the synthesized carbon dots, activated by specific wavelengths, suggests that such marked banknotes and cards have potential for preventing counterfeiting. Zhang et al. synthesized carbon dots using a hydrothermal method, allowing for tunable luminescent colors based on the types of reagents used [39]. They achieved blue, cyan, and green luminescence. By utilizing these three types of carbon dots, they fabricated films that, when irradiated with a UV lamp, displayed various shapes along with their inherent luminescent colors. This demonstrated the potential application of these films in anti-counterfeiting measures.

The applicability of CDs in solution as photodetectors for detecting heavy metal ions, Fig. 10 (a) shows photographs of a pure CDs solution, a solution with added metal ions, and a mixture of the two under UV lamp irradiation. While there was no significant change in the luminescence of the solutions observed with the naked eye under UV lamp irradiation, the luminescence intensity of the solution mixed with Hg ions was noticeably reduced, as shown in the graph below, and this decrease was observable with the naked eye. After confirming the significant reduction in luminescence intensity due to Hg ions, the changes in luminescence intensity according to the concentration of Hg2+ ions were observed, as shown in Fig. 10(b).

The CDs solution mixed with Hg2+ ions turned almost transparent under normal light and lost its characteristic cyan luminescence almost entirely when irradiated with a UV lamp. As shown in the graph below, the PL luminescence intensity decreased as the concentration of metal ions increased, with a particularly sharp decline starting at 40 μM.

To investigate the impact of Hg2+ concentration on the CD solution, we measured the luminescence spectra and intensity of the solution containing Hg2+. These results are illustrated in Fig. 10(b) and Fig. S5, respectively. As shown in Fig. 10(b), the primary emission wavelength of the CDs remained constant despite changes in Hg2+ concentration. However, Fig. S4 reveals that the luminescence intensity diminished as the Hg2+ concentration increased. Quenching efficiency was evaluated using a Stern-Volmer plot according to the following equation [41]:
(4)
(
I 0
/ I )
‐ 1 = Ksv
∗
Q
where I
0 and I are the luminescence intensities of the CDs in the presence and absence of Hg2+, respectively; Q is the concentration of Hg2+ ions; and K
SV is the quenching constant. The quenching constant was calculated with a correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.98548). The test performance of the Hg2+ concentration, the limit of detection (LoD) was calculated using the three-sigma method [41]:
(5)
L o D = 3 σ / S

The parameter σ represents the standard deviation of the luminescence intensity, and S is the slope obtained from the linear fitting results. The limit of detection (LoD) was found to be 0.1516 μM. Fig. S6 illustrates the luminescence processes for both the pristine and Hg2+-ion-containing CD solutions. In the luminescence mechanism, the pristine CDs absorbed incident light, which excited electrons from the valence band to a higher energy state. These excited electrons then transitioned to the emission state, resulting in light emission (luminescence) as they returned to the valence band. When Hg2+ ions were present in the CDs, the overall absorption increased due to additional light absorption by the Hg2+ ions. This led to partial re-emission of the absorbed light to the Hg2+ ions through the inner filtration effect. Consequently, the intensity of excitation light reaching the CDs decreased, thereby reducing the luminescence intensity in the Hg2+-containing CD solution. As shown in Fig. 11 , the synthesized carbon dots showed a decrease in the luminescence intensity of the PL spectrum and a decrease in the luminescence lifetime depending on the concentration of mercury ions. Previous references demonstrating the detection of Hg2+ using carbon dots are illustrated in Table S2. These studies have shown the ability to detect Hg2+ within a similar range to our work and at even lower concentrations. Gao et al. observed changes in luminescent properties by reacting carbon dots emitting at 440 nm with Hg2⁺ ions. They reported that the luminescence was significantly quenched at 10 mmol/L Hg2⁺ due to non-radiative electron/hole recombination via effective electron transfer and the conversion of the spirolactam-structured functional group (–CONH–) to an open-ring amide by Hg2+ [42]. Additionally, the quenching of luminescence intensity and lifetime in CDs caused by Hg2⁺ is primarily due to non-radiative electron transfer, chemical interactions altering surface passivation, functional group modifications, and spin-orbit coupling effects. These processes collectively disrupt the radiative decay pathways, leading to significant fluorescence quenching.
they have demonstrated the potential use of carbon dot solutions as optical detectors.

Piperazine and phthalic acid were dissolved in water to synthesize carbon dots that emit a strong green color through an easy method. The synthesized carbon dots could be used both in powder and aqueous solution forms, and their successful synthesis was confirmed through various analyses: XRD patterns showed the peak signal of the (002) plane, Raman shifts exhibited the signals of D and G bands, and XPS revealed binding energy peaks of C, N, and O 1s. When excited with UV light, the carbon dots emitted a cyan spectrum with a broad peak ranging from 450 nm to 650 nm. Although the peak position did not change with different excitation wavelengths, the emission intensity varied, showing the strongest spectrum when excited at 420 nm. To demonstrate the versatility of these carbon dots, they were mixed with PDMS in powder form and fabricated into various shapes. The resulting composites were flexible, easily bent, folded, and rolled by hand. These composites were subjected to harsh condition tests, including resistance to moisture, temperature, and UV quenching, to assess their applicability to LED chips. The composite sheets exposed to harsh conditions for about 40 h retained their characteristic cyan emission despite a 50 % reduction in emission intensity, and they maintained emission even at 200 °C. Additionally, when applied directly to blue LEDs, they reduced the blue wavelength by 95 %, indicating potential for eye protection. In another application, carbon dot powder was sprinkled onto glass substrates with fingerprints, and UV lamp irradiation allowed for the observation of unique fingerprint features, demonstrating potential for use in crime prevention. The carbon dots were also used as ink on banknotes and plastic cards for anti-counterfeiting purposes. Furthermore, the carbon dots showed a significant decrease in emission intensity when reacting with Hg2+, indicating their potential application as optical detectors for Hg2+ detection.

Jae Yong Jung: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Jin Young Park: Software, Formal analysis, Data curation. Sung Jun Park: Data curation, Formal analysis. Hyun Kyoung Yang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (grant number: RS-2023-00240874, RS-2023-00237313, and RS-2023-00246326)

Multimedia component 2 Multimedia component 2

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtchem.2025.102555.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots: a New type of carbon-based nanomaterial with Wide applications
ACS Cent. Sci.
